extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin (PT) is a thermal incubator system comprising an insulated box with a heatbed, sensors, a controller, and a software system for communication and simulation. It operates in a controlled thermal environment, with the heatbed's thermal inertia being a key characteristic. The system is a Cyber-Physical System (CPS) integrating physical processes (heat transfer) with computational control (temperature regulation). The PT's behavior is modeled using simulation models with 2 or 4 free parameters to predict thermal dynamics.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides real-time visualization of incubator status via dashboard plotting of time-series data. It enables on-demand calibration using automated parameter estimation. Anomaly detection and heatbed state estimation are achieved through Kalman filters correlating measurement data with non-measured heatbed temperatures. What-if simulations use co-simulations with historical/real-time PT data to optimize controller parameters. A self-adaptation loop dynamically adjusts the controller when new objects (e.g., cold water) are introduced, involving data collection, model recalibration, and controller reconfiguration.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: RabbitMQ [https://www.rabbitmq.com/] serves as the communication server for message brokering between controller, low-level driver, and DT components. The low-level driver interacts with the PT, implements safety protocols, and uploads temperature data to the communication server. The controller is a bang-bang controller variant managing temperature regulation. Simulation models (2/4 parameter) are calibrated using experimental data. A time-series database stores sensor data for visualization. The Raspberry Pi hosts the controller, with the low-level driver periodically reading temperature data and executing commands via RabbitMQ.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time synchronization between physical and digital components via RabbitMQ message broker, with Kalman filter processing of historical/real-time data for anomaly detection. Self-adaptation loop requires real-time state estimation and control parameter optimization.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Multiple digital twin instances (e.g., two simulation models with 2/4 free parameters) operating within a centralized architecture. Coordination mechanisms include shared communication server (RabbitMQ) and parameter synchronization across models.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestrated integration of cyber-physical components (controller, low-level driver, communication server) with modeling/simulation (Kalman filter, co-simulations) and monitoring/optimization (dashboard visualization, fault detection). Architecture includes parameter calibration and adaptive control loops.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with open-source hardware/software ecosystem via RabbitMQ for data exchange. Leverages industry standards for message brokering and interoperability, with potential connections to enterprise systems through shared data protocols.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin models include behavioral (differential equations [3, Section 5]), analytical (Model A/B with parameters C_air, G_box, C_heater, G_heater), and geometric models. Data sources: sensors on Physical Twin (PT) for real-time monitoring. Data management: time series database for dashboard visualization. Models are calibrated using algorithms (e.g., Kalman filter [16]) to update parameters (e.g., C_air=486.12 J/K, G_box=0.856 J/K for Model B). Models are integrated with simulation (co-simulations with PT data) and optimization (controller parameter tuning).
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators include heater for temperature control. Control mechanisms: PID controller for maintaining setpoints, remote interface via Controller Area Network (CAN) for real-time adjustments. Actuation range: temperature regulation within safe limits (e.g., cooling to safe temperatures before heating). Safety constraints: prevent overheating via anomaly detection. Addressing mechanisms: controller communication protocols for dynamic reconfiguration.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors: temperature sensors for heatbed and environment. Measurement capabilities: thermal inertia prediction. Spatial distribution: heatbed and ambient areas. Sampling frequency: real-time data collection for calibration (e.g., Model B parameters). Data transmission: high-frequency updates to time series database. Accuracy: calibrated parameters (e.g., C_air=616.56 J/K for Model A). Precision: quantified via calibration experiments (Fig. 13).
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity: Model B (4 parameters) outperforms Model A (2 parameters) in predicting heatbed inertia. extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from physical sensors (e.g., DS18S20 temperature sensors) to digital twin via RabbitMQ. Data quality assurance: calibration algorithms update models with best parameter estimates. Preprocessing: temperature data uploaded to communication server. extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands (e.g., fan/heatbed control) flow from digital twin to physical twin via RabbitMQ. Reliability: RabbitMQ's mature, lightweight design ensures fault tolerance. Redundancy: not explicitly mentioned. Security protocols: safe protocol in low-level driver for device safety.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Hosting platform: Raspberry Pi (edge device). Computational requirements: Raspberry Pi handles control logic and data processing. Lifecycle governance involves integrating models into the DT framework (section 2) and continuous calibration/update processes (section 4).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The process uses simulation modeling (two models: 2 and 4 free parameters), calibration algorithms (section 3.3), and co-simulation for what-if scenarios (section 4). Quality assurance includes model validation via thermal inertia prediction experiments. Version control is implied through iterative model refinement. Evolution strategies involve dynamic reconfiguration of Kalman filters (section 4) and controller parameter optimization.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support includes real-time visualization of incubator status (dashboard via time series database), anomaly detection using Kalman filters (section 4), and what-if simulations for controller optimization. Analytics involve correlating measured data with non-measured heatbed temperatures. User interaction is via dashboards and automated controller reconfiguration. Confidence measures are derived from Kalman filter state estimation accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Complies with open-source standards (RabbitMQ [4] for message brokering, MQTT/AMQP protocols). No explicit mention of industry-specific standards (e.g., ISO/IEC) or certification frameworks. Interoperability is achieved through RabbitMQ's message-passing architecture and modular component design (section 2.2).

### **2 Incubator Case Study**

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **5 Digital Twinning Goals**

In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator
case study. This constitutes our future work.
Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables
**Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series
database, and plot the data into a dashboard.
**On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process
described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented
in [15].
**Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a
Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the
measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature.
**What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the
historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It
can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8.
**Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT.
We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is
placed in the incubator, the DT will:

1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected
by the Kalman filter);
2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down
to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time).
3. configure the controller for the new experiment;
4. gather the experiment data;
5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment;
6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters;
7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and
8. finally re-configure the controller.

### **5 Digital Twinning Goals**

In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator
case study. This constitutes our future work.
Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables
**Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series
database, and plot the data into a dashboard.
**On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process
described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented
in [15].
**Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a
Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the
measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature.
**What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the
historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It
can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8.
**Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT.
We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is
placed in the incubator, the DT will:

1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected
by the Kalman filter);
2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down
to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time).
3. configure the controller for the new experiment;
4. gather the experiment data;
5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment;
6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters;
7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and
8. finally re-configure the controller.

February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **5 Digital Twinning Goals**

In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator
case study. This constitutes our future work.
Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables
**Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series
database, and plot the data into a dashboard.
**On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process
described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented
in [15].
**Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a
Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the
measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature.
**What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the
historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It
can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8.
**Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT.
We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is
placed in the incubator, the DT will:

1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected
by the Kalman filter);
2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down
to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time).
3. configure the controller for the new experiment;
4. gather the experiment data;
5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment;
6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters;
7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and
8. finally re-configure the controller.

The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].

how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.

### **5 Digital Twinning Goals**

In this section, we sketch the goals of the digital twinning process for the incubator
case study. This constitutes our future work.
Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables
**Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series
database, and plot the data into a dashboard.
**On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process
described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented
in [15].
**Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a
Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the
measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature.
**What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the
historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It
can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8.
**Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT.
We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is
placed in the incubator, the DT will:

1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected
by the Kalman filter);
2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down
to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time).
3. configure the controller for the new experiment;
4. gather the experiment data;
5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment;
6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters;
7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and
8. finally re-configure the controller.

Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,

Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,

DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

processes [1].

directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.
